content.text:
Perfect! I now have all the detailed information about where skills are stored and their format. Let me provide you with a comprehensive answer.

## Where User-Invocable Skills Are Stored

Claude Code skills are stored in three possible locations depending on their scope:

### Directory Locations

| Scope      | Path                                      | Applies To                |
|------------|-------------------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Personal   | `~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`  | All your projects         |
| Project    | `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`    | Current project only      |
| Enterprise | Configured via managed settings           | All users in organization |

### File Format

Each skill is a directory containing at minimum:

**Required file:**
- `SKILL.md` - The main skill file with YAML frontmatter and markdown instructions

**Optional supporting files:**
```
my-skill/
├── SKILL.md           # Main instructions (required)
├── reference.md       # Detailed documentation
├── examples.md        # Usage examples
├── template.md        # Template for Claude to fill in
└── scripts/
    └── helper.py      # Utility scripts
```

### SKILL.md Format

The `SKILL.md` file has two parts:

```yaml
---
name: my-skill-name
description: What this skill does and when to use it
disable-model-invocation: false
user-invocable: true
allowed-tools: Read, Grep
---

Your markdown instructions here that Claude will follow when the skill is invoked.
```

### Key Frontmatter Fields

| Field                      | Purpose                                                           |
|----------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `name`                     | Display name used for `/skill-name` command (lowercase, hyphens)  |
| `description`              | When to use this skill (Claude uses this to auto-invoke)          |
| `disable-model-invocation` | Set to `true` for manual-only skills (like `/deploy`)             |
| `user-invocable`           | Set to `false` to hide from menu but let Claude use automatically |
| `allowed-tools`            | Tools Claude can use without permission when skill is active      |
| `context`                  | Set to `fork` to run in isolated subagent                         |
| `agent`                    | Which subagent type to use with `context: fork`                  |

### Example: Create a Simple Skill

To create your first skill:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/explain-code
```

Then create `~/.claude/skills/explain-code/SKILL.md`:

```yaml
---
name: explain-code
description: Explains code with visual diagrams and analogies
---

When explaining code, include:

1. An analogy comparing the code to something from everyday life
2. An ASCII diagram showing the flow or structure
3. Step-by-step walkthrough of what happens
4. A common gotcha or misconception
```

Now you can invoke it with `/explain-code` or let Claude use it automatically when you ask "How does this code work?"

### Priority When Names Conflict

When the same skill exists at multiple levels: **Enterprise > Personal > Project** (higher priority wins). Plugin skills use a `plugin-name:skill-name` namespace so they never conflict.

For more details on advanced patterns, arguments, and dynamic context injection, see the [Claude Code skills documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md).

toolUseResult.prompt: Where are user-invocable skills stored for Claude Code? What is the file format and directory location for defining custom skills that can be invoked with /skill-name syntax? Please provide the exact path and format.

content.text:
Perfect! I now have all the detailed information about where skills are stored and their format. Let me provide you with a comprehensive answer.

## Where User-Invocable Skills Are Stored

Claude Code skills are stored in three possible locations depending on their scope:

### Directory Locations

| Scope      | Path                                      | Applies To                |
|------------|-------------------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Personal   | `~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`  | All your projects         |
| Project    | `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`    | Current project only      |
| Enterprise | Configured via managed settings           | All users in organization |

### File Format

Each skill is a directory containing at minimum:

**Required file:**
- `SKILL.md` - The main skill file with YAML frontmatter and markdown instructions

**Optional supporting files:**
```
my-skill/
├── SKILL.md           # Main instructions (required)
├── reference.md       # Detailed documentation
├── examples.md        # Usage examples
├── template.md        # Template for Claude to fill in
└── scripts/
    └── helper.py      # Utility scripts
```

### SKILL.md Format

The `SKILL.md` file has two parts:

```yaml
---
name: my-skill-name
description: What this skill does and when to use it
disable-model-invocation: false
user-invocable: true
allowed-tools: Read, Grep
---

Your markdown instructions here that Claude will follow when the skill is invoked.
```

### Key Frontmatter Fields

| Field                      | Purpose                                                           |
|----------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `name`                     | Display name used for `/skill-name` command (lowercase, hyphens)  |
| `description`              | When to use this skill (Claude uses this to auto-invoke)          |
| `disable-model-invocation` | Set to `true` for manual-only skills (like `/deploy`)             |
| `user-invocable`           | Set to `false` to hide from menu but let Claude use automatically |
| `allowed-tools`            | Tools Claude can use without permission when skill is active      |
| `context`                  | Set to `fork` to run in isolated subagent                         |
| `agent`                    | Which subagent type to use with `context: fork`                  |

### Example: Create a Simple Skill

To create your first skill:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/explain-code
```

Then create `~/.claude/skills/explain-code/SKILL.md`:

```yaml
---
name: explain-code
description: Explains code with visual diagrams and analogies
---

When explaining code, include:

1. An analogy comparing the code to something from everyday life
2. An ASCII diagram showing the flow or structure
3. Step-by-step walkthrough of what happens
4. A common gotcha or misconception
```

Now you can invoke it with `/explain-code` or let Claude use it automatically when you ask "How does this code work?"

### Priority When Names Conflict

When the same skill exists at multiple levels: **Enterprise > Personal > Project** (higher priority wins). Plugin skills use a `plugin-name:skill-name` namespace so they never conflict.

For more details on advanced patterns, arguments, and dynamic context injection, see the [Claude Code skills documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md).

content.thinking:
Now I know the format. Skills are stored in `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` for project-specific skills, or `~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` for personal skills. The user wants a skill for processing conjecture files from the `../erdos/conjectures/` directory.

Let me create a project-level skill (in `.claude/skills/`) since it's specific to this project. The skill should:

1. Read a source file from `../erdos/conjectures/<N>.lean`
2. Transform it to match the AGENTS.md conventions
3. Write it to `FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/<N>.lean`


4. Handle transformations like adding copyright headers, updating imports, wrapping in namespaces, adding attributes, renaming theorems, and fixing sorry statements.

Now I need to create the skill file in the project's `.claude/skills/` directory.

input.content:
---
name: formalize-erdos
description: Copy and reformat an Erdős conjecture file from ../erdos/conjectures/<N>.lean into FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/<N>.lean, applying all AGENTS.md conventions.
user-invocable: true
---

The user has invoked this skill with an Erdős problem number (e.g. `/formalize-erdos 42`).
Extract the problem number N from the arguments.

Follow these steps exactly:

## Step 1 — Read source and reference files

Read both of these files in parallel:
- `../erdos/conjectures/<N>.lean` — the source file to transform
- `FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/1.lean` — a reference for the expected style

Also check whether `FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/<N>.lean` already exists. If it does,
tell the user and stop — do not overwrite existing work.

## Step 2 — Determine the category

Inspect the source file's docstring and decide the correct `category` attribute:
- If the problem is described as solved (e.g. mentions a proof, a prover, a year of resolution),
  use `@[category research solved, AMS ...]`
- If the problem is still open, use `@[category research open, AMS ...]`
- Choose the AMS subject number(s) from the table in AGENTS.md based on the mathematical content.
  Number theory → 11, Combinatorics → 5, etc. Multiple subjects are allowed: `AMS 5 11`.

## Step 3 — Write the output file

Write `FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/<N>.lean` with the following transformations applied:

1. **Copyright header** — prepend exactly:
   ```
   /-
   Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

   Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
   you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
   You may obtain a copy of the License at

       https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

   Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
   distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
   WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
   See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
   limitations under the License.
   -/
   ```

2. **Import** — replace all `import` lines with a single line:
   ```
   import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
   ```

3. **Module docstring** — add immediately after the import:
   ```
   /-!
   # Erdős Problem <N>

   *Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/<N>](https://www.erdosproblems.com/<N>)
   -/
   ```
   Preserve any additional descriptive content from the source file's top-level docstring
   if it adds meaningful context, but do not duplicate what is already in theorem docstrings.

4. **Namespace** — wrap all declarations in `namespace Erdos<N> ... end Erdos<N>`.

5. **Category and AMS attributes** — add `@[category ..., AMS ...]` immediately before
   each theorem or lemma that does not already have one.

6. **Theorem names** — rename the primary theorem from whatever it is in the source
   (e.g. `erdos_problem_2`, `main`, etc.) to `erdos_<N>`. Rename variants to
   `erdos_<N>.variants.<description>` following the pattern in `1.lean`.

7. **`sorry` style** — replace bare `sorry` with `by sorry` in all theorem bodies.

8. **Docstring math** — convert inline backtick math like `` `n ≡ a (mod m)` `` to
   LaTeX `$n \equiv a \pmod{m}$` in docstrings where appropriate.

9. **`def` definitions** — keep any helper definitions. If they have docstrings, ensure
   math is in LaTeX. Do NOT add `@[category]` attributes to `def`s, only to theorems/lemmas.

## Step 4 — Report

After writing the file, briefly summarize:
- The file path written
- The category assigned and why
- The AMS subject(s) chosen and why
- Any judgment calls made (e.g. ambiguous open/solved status)
